title: Caloric Surplus Calculator for Muscle Gain | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Ideal Caloric Surplus for Building Muscle
description: Calculate your ideal caloric surplus for muscle gain without excess fat. Based on TDEE and experience level · Free, fast & accurate
intro: A caloric surplus calculator for muscle gain determines exactly how many calories above your maintenance level (TDEE) you need to eat each day to maximize lean muscle growth while minimizing fat accumulation. It uses the Mifflin-St Jeor equation to estimate your Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR), multiplies it by an activity factor to get your Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE), then adds a research-backed surplus of 200–500 kcal/day depending on your training experience. Use this tool whenever you're starting a structured bulking phase, adjusting macros mid-cycle, or transitioning from a cut to a lean bulk.
keyTakeaway: **Daily Calories for Bulking = TDEE + Surplus**, where TDEE = BMR × Activity Factor, BMR (men) = 10×weight(kg) + 6.25×height(cm) − 5×age + 5, and the surplus ranges from **200 kcal/day (advanced lifters)** to **500 kcal/day (beginners)** to gain 0.25–1 kg of lean mass per month.
useCases: A 24-year-old male beginner (75 kg, 178 cm) starting his first structured bulking program who needs to know exactly how many calories to eat to gain ~1 kg of muscle per month without excessive fat gain.

An intermediate lifter (82 kg, 5 training years) transitioning from a 12-week cut back to a lean bulk and wants to set the minimum surplus needed to resume muscle protein synthesis without regaining fat rapidly.
A female athlete (62 kg, 165 cm, highly active) preparing for an off-season hypertrophy block who needs to calculate her elevated TDEE accurately before adding a conservative 200–250 kcal surplus.
A personal trainer building individualized meal plans for multiple clients at different training experience levels and needing repeatable, formula-based calorie targets for each bulking phase.

faq.q: How large should my caloric surplus be to build muscle without gaining too much fat?
faq.a: Research and practitioner consensus recommend 200–500 kcal/day above your TDEE depending on experience. Beginners can use 400–500 kcal/day because their muscle protein synthesis capacity is highest; advanced lifters should stay at 200–300 kcal/day. A surplus above 500 kcal/day has not been shown to increase muscle gain rates but does linearly increase fat storage.

faq.q: How accurate is the Mifflin-St Jeor equation for calculating TDEE?
faq.a: The Mifflin-St Jeor equation predicts resting metabolic rate within ±10% for approximately 82% of healthy adults, according to a 2005 review in the Journal of the American Dietetic Association. It outperforms the older Harris-Benedict equation, which was developed on a smaller, less diverse 1919 dataset. Individual variation (genetics, lean mass, thyroid function) means real-world TDEE can still deviate ±15%, so recalibrating based on 2–3 weeks of actual weight tracking is recommended.

faq.q: How much muscle can I realistically gain per month on a caloric surplus?
faq.a: Natural muscle gain potential is well-documented: beginners (0–1 year) can gain roughly 0.75–1.0 kg/month; intermediates (1–3 years) about 0.5–0.75 kg/month; advanced lifters (3–5 years) around 0.25–0.5 kg/month; and elite lifters may see only 0.1–0.25 kg/month. These rates reflect lean tissue only — scale weight will increase faster due to water, glycogen, and gut content. Women typically gain at 50–70% of these rates due to lower testosterone levels.

faq.q: Should women use a different surplus strategy than men?
faq.a: The formula is the same, but women's baseline TDEE is lower (the Mifflin-St Jeor equation subtracts 161 kcal for females), and their muscle gain ceiling is roughly 50–70% of men's due to lower average testosterone (15–70 ng/dL in women vs. 300–1,000 ng/dL in men per NIH reference ranges). A surplus of 200–350 kcal/day is typically sufficient for female lifters, with the lower end appropriate for intermediate-to-advanced trainees.

faq.q: What is the difference between a lean bulk and a dirty bulk?
faq.a: A lean bulk uses a controlled surplus of 200–500 kcal/day targeting a muscle-to-fat gain ratio of roughly 1:1 or better; a dirty bulk uses an unrestricted surplus (often 700–1,500+ kcal/day) in the mistaken belief that more calories accelerate muscle growth beyond its physiological ceiling. Studies consistently show dirty bulking results in disproportionate fat gain without meaningfully faster muscle synthesis, requiring longer and harder cutting phases afterward to restore body composition.

faq.q: How do I know if my caloric surplus is correct after a few weeks?
faq.a: Track weekly average body weight (weigh daily, average 7 days) and expect a gain of 0.25–0.5 kg/week on a beginner bulk or 0.1–0.25 kg/week for intermediate/advanced. If you gain faster, reduce the surplus by 100–150 kcal/day; if you gain nothing or lose weight over two consecutive weeks, increase by 100–150 kcal/day. Simultaneously, monitor lifts — progressive overload (adding ~2.5–5 kg to compound lifts per month) is the primary behavioral indicator that muscle is being built.

faq.q: Do I need to eat in a surplus on rest days?
faq.a: Muscle protein synthesis (MPS) remains elevated for 24–48 hours after resistance training, meaning your body is actively building muscle on rest days too. Most sports nutritionists recommend maintaining close to the same caloric intake on rest days (within ±100–200 kcal) rather than cycling dramatically, as consistent nutrient availability supports ongoing MPS. Some advanced practitioners use calorie cycling (higher on training days, lower on rest days) but evidence for superior outcomes versus flat intake is limited.

faq.q: Does protein intake affect how many calories I need for muscle gain?
faq.a: Yes — adequate protein is a prerequisite for the caloric surplus to translate into muscle rather than fat. The USDA Dietary Guidelines and sports nutrition research recommend 1.6–2.2 g of protein per kg of body weight per day for maximizing muscle protein synthesis (e.g., 120–165 g/day for a 75 kg person). Protein also has the highest thermic effect of food (TEF) at ~25–30% vs. 5–10% for carbohydrates and 0–3% for fats, meaning high-protein diets slightly elevate daily calorie burn, effectively requiring you to eat a bit more to maintain the same surplus.

sources.name: NIH — Dietary Reference Intakes for Energy (NASEM 2023)

explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The calculator runs a three-step pipeline: BMR → TDEE → Bulking Calories.

**Step 1 — Basal Metabolic Rate (Mifflin-St Jeor equation)**

```
Men:   BMR = (10 × weight_kg) + (6.25 × height_cm) − (5 × age) + 5
Women: BMR = (10 × weight_kg) + (6.25 × height_cm) − (5 × age) − 161
```

**Step 2 — Total Daily Energy Expenditure (TDEE)**

```
TDEE = BMR × Activity Factor
```

**Step 3 — Bulking Calories & Recommended Surplus**

```
Bulking Calories = TDEE + Surplus
Surplus (beginner)     = 400–500 kcal/day  → ~0.75–1.0 kg lean mass/month
Surplus (intermediate) = 300–400 kcal/day  → ~0.5–0.75 kg lean mass/month
Surplus (advanced)     = 200–300 kcal/day  → ~0.25–0.5 kg lean mass/month
```

> **Why Mifflin-St Jeor?** A 2005 meta-analysis published in the *Journal of the American Dietetic Association* found it to be the most accurate predictive equation for healthy adults, within ±10% of measured RMR in 82% of subjects — outperforming the older Harris-Benedict formula.

---

## Reference Table

### Activity Factors (PAL Multipliers)

| Activity Level | Description | Multiplier |
|---|---|---|
| Sedentary | Desk job, no structured exercise | 1.2 |
| Lightly Active | 1–3 days/week light training | 1.375 |
| Moderately Active | 3–5 days/week moderate training | 1.55 |
| Very Active | 6–7 days/week intense training | 1.725 |
| Extremely Active | 2× daily training or physical labor job | 1.9 |

### Recommended Surplus by Training Experience

| Experience Level | Years Lifting | Surplus (kcal/day) | Expected Muscle Gain/Month |
|---|---|---|---|
| Beginner | 0–1 year | 400–500 | 0.75–1.0 kg |
| Intermediate | 1–3 years | 300–400 | 0.5–0.75 kg |
| Advanced | 3–5 years | 200–300 | 0.25–0.5 kg |
| Elite | 5+ years | 100–200 | 0.1–0.25 kg |

> These muscle gain rates align with the commonly cited Lyle McDonald model, which estimates natural muscle gain potential decreases roughly 50% per year of serious training.

---

## Typical Examples

### Example 1 — Male Beginner
- **Profile:** Male, 22 years old, 75 kg, 178 cm, moderately active (3–5×/week), 0 years lifting
- **BMR:** (10 × 75) + (6.25 × 178) − (5 × 22) + 5 = 750 + 1112.5 − 110 + 5 = **1,757.5 kcal**
- **TDEE:** 1,757.5 × 1.55 = **2,724 kcal/day**
- **Surplus:** +450 kcal (beginner range)
- **Bulking Target:** **3,174 kcal/day**
- **Expected Gain:** ~0.9 kg lean mass/month

### Example 2 — Female Intermediate
- **Profile:** Female, 28 years old, 62 kg, 165 cm, very active (6×/week), 2 years lifting
- **BMR:** (10 × 62) + (6.25 × 165) − (5 × 28) − 161 = 620 + 1031.25 − 140 − 161 = **1,350.25 kcal**
- **TDEE:** 1,350.25 × 1.725 = **2,329 kcal/day**
- **Surplus:** +325 kcal (intermediate range)
- **Bulking Target:** **2,654 kcal/day**
- **Expected Gain:** ~0.6 kg lean mass/month

### Example 3 — Advanced Male Lifter
- **Profile:** Male, 32 years old, 90 kg, 182 cm, very active, 5 years lifting
- **BMR:** (10 × 90) + (6.25 × 182) − (5 × 32) + 5 = 900 + 1137.5 − 160 + 5 = **1,882.5 kcal**
- **TDEE:** 1,882.5 × 1.725 = **3,247 kcal/day**
- **Surplus:** +200 kcal (advanced range)
- **Bulking Target:** **3,447 kcal/day**
- **Expected Gain:** ~0.3 kg lean mass/month

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using too large a surplus regardless of experience.** Many beginners add 1,000+ kcal/day ("dirty bulk"), expecting faster muscle growth. But muscle protein synthesis has a ceiling — roughly 0.25–1 kg/month for natural lifters. Excess calories beyond the physiological cap are stored as fat, not muscle.

2. **Ignoring the activity multiplier.** Using a sedentary multiplier (1.2) when you train 5×/week underestimates TDEE by 400–600 kcal/day, making your "surplus" actually a deficit. Always reassess your activity level honestly.

3. **Not adjusting surplus as you gain experience.** A 500 kcal surplus is appropriate in month 1 but excessive by year 3. Advanced lifters who maintain a beginner-sized surplus will accumulate disproportionate fat gain because their muscle gain ceiling drops significantly.

4. **Confusing body weight gain with muscle gain.** On a proper bulk, expect total scale weight to increase ~2–4× faster than actual lean mass due to added glycogen (~3 g water per gram), water retention from higher sodium/carb intake, and gut content. Do not panic and reduce calories after seeing normal scale fluctuations.

5. **Forgetting to recalculate as body weight changes.** Every 3–5 kg of body weight gain increases BMR by ~30–50 kcal/day. Failing to update your target means your surplus silently shrinks over weeks, stalling muscle gain.
